title: “Too many symbolic links” loading ignores
author: chrisisbd
body:
### What happened?

I'm running syncthing version 2.1.2 on Debian 13.

If I make the .stignore file a symbolic link to a file (say stignore) in the same directory as .stignore, i.e. a very simple symbolic link I get the following errors:-

```
2026-07-10 11:39:19: Failed to load ignores (error="open /home/chris/.share/.stignore: too many levels of symbolic links" log.pkg=model)

2026-07-10 11:39:19: Failed initial scan (error="loading ignores: open /home/chris/.share/.stignore: too many levels of symbolic links" folder.label=share folder.id=js5va-zt99v folder.type=sendreceive log.pkg=model)

```
The symbolic link is as follows:-

```
chris@q957$ cd /home/chris/.share
/home/chris/.share
chris@q957$ ls -l .stignore stignore
lrwxrwxrwx 1 chris chris  8 Jul 10 11:37 .stignore -> stignore
-rw-rw-r-- 1 chris chris 12 Jul 10 11:31 stignore
chris@q957$ 

```

### Syncthing version

v2.1.2

### Platform & operating system

Debian Linux

### Browser version

_No response_

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

title: v2.1.1 to v2.1.2 upgrade database migration hang
author: arsinclair
body:
### What happened?

I've just upgraded from v2.1.1 to v2.1.2 within the Web UI and the Syncthing went down to reboot. However, it didn't start properly after the reboot. The https link is no longer accessible, the browser error is `ERR_SSL_PROTOCOL_ERROR`. If I go to the http link I can see this:

```
*** Database migration in progress ***

2026-07-10 17:34:02 INF syncthing v2.1.2 "Hafnium Hornet" (go1.26.5 linux-amd64) builder@github.syncthing.net 2026-06-26 05:22:40 UTC [stguiassets] (log.pkg=main)
2026-07-10 17:34:02 INF Applying database migration (path=folder.0006-hmu9jfta.db script=06-zero-size-dirs.sql log.pkg=db/sqlite)
2026-07-10 17:34:02 INF Applying database migration (path=folder.0003-xapq42xi.db script=06-zero-size-dirs.sql log.pkg=db/sqlite)
2026-07-10 17:34:07 INF Starting temporary GUI/API during migration (address=0.0.0.0:8384 log.pkg=main)
```

It's been 15 minutes now and nothing is happening. Why is it stuck? Can I restart it? I'm afraid if I restart it, the database might get corrupt if there's truly some migration in progress.

This was run on the source machine, and the same migration was done on the target machine (so database size should be roughly the same) but on the target machine the whole upgrade process succeeded within 1 minute.

### Syncthing version

v2.1.2

### Platform & operating system

Synology DSM

### Browser version

_No response_

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

title: 两个服务器之间的相互备份，进度显示异常
author: leixuok
body:
### What happened?

[svr3100-33-200 _ Syncthing.pdf](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/29727616/svr3100-33-200._.Syncthing.pdf)
[svr5500-33-199 _ Syncthing.pdf](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/29727617/svr5500-33-199._.Syncthing.pdf)
我创建了两个服务器之间的相互备份，他们是两个不同的文件夹。我首先创建的是5500的备份，然后他已经开始了备份。之后我创建了3100的备份，接下来好像他停止了5500的备份，而新开始的3100的备份，最后是3100的备份先完成。在上述背景情况之下，在5500服务器这一端，界面上的远程设备同步进度，数据一直不变。而在3100服务器这一端的界面上，5500备份文件夹同步中一直显示0%，而右下角的远程设备里面的进度没有任何显示，只显示了一个最新。

I set up mutual backups between two servers, using two different folders. I first created the backup for 5500, and it had already started running. After that, I created the backup for 3100. It then seemed that the 5500 backup was stopped, and the newly started 3100 backup took over. In the end, the 3100 backup completed first.

Given the above situation, on the 5500 server side, the remote device sync progress displayed on the interface remained unchanged. Meanwhile, on the 3100 server side, the interface showed that the 5500 backup folder was syncing but always at 0%, and the progress bar for the remote device in the bottom-right corner showed no progress at all—only the word "Latest" was displayed.

### Syncthing version

v2.1.1

### Platform & operating system

Linux (64-bit Intel/AMD)

### Browser version

Google Chrome  149.0.7827.201（正式版本） （64 位）

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

title: Performance regression: very slow scanning
author: iyanmv
body: ### What happened? Since two or three releases ago (don't know exactly when this changed, but probably [v2.1.0](https://github.com/syncthing/syncthing/releases/tag/v2.1.0)) scanning is terribly slow. In the past, scanning would take a few seconds (way less than a minute). Now on both my PC and laptop this process can take way over 10 minutes. title: Expose CLI option for configuration/health check
author: nf-brentsaner
body:
### Feature description

There should be a way to validate the configuration (and *check*, but not generate, the device cert/key).

Proposed (respectively):

* `syncthing cli show config-health` (check config file syntax correctness)
* `syncthing cli show key-health` (check for presence of, and valid form of, `cert.pem` and `key.pem`)

Further recommendations:

* It MUST NOT require syncthing to be running.
* It MUST evaluate `--config`, `$STCONFDIR`, `--home`, and `$STHOMEDIR` in the exact same way invoking e.g. `syncthing serve`, `syncthing cli config ...`, etc. does.
* It MAY offer an option (via an optional switch e.g. `--fix` or `--write`) to fix issues if found, but MUST NOT default to this behavior.
* It MUST return non-zero exit status if any issue is found, and SHOULD print to `STDERR` the issue(s) found.

### Problem or use case

When managing widely-deployed Syncthing instances, primarily, it's important to ensure that Syncthing has a way of validating the configuration.

Similarly, I was able to set invalid listening addresses via `syncthing cli config ...` by setting a device's address as its IP with no `tcp://` - not only did `syncthing cli config` let me do this, but I had no idea it was invalid at the time as the only way to actually see that it was throwing an error was on the web UI.
(Yes, yes; the docs specify the correct syntax, but I'm surprised that this was not only not validated on input from `syncthing cli config` but there is no way from the CLI to confirm/validate the config.)

### Alternatives or workarounds

N/A; there is no way to validate configuration file without trying to run syncthing and checking logs/viewing web UI (assuming it starts).

title: Option to limit disk I/O throughput (maxDiskIOKbps)
author: ViPiC-Ru
body:
### Feature description

A global configuration option `maxDiskIOKbps` that caps the total disk I/O throughput used by all folder operations combined (scanning and syncing), analogous to the existing `maxSendKbps` / `maxRecvKbps` network bandwidth limits.

When set to a non-zero value, a shared token-bucket rate limiter would throttle:

- disk reads during folder scanning (file hashing)
- disk reads during sync (local block copies)
- disk writes during sync (writing received blocks to temp files)

Setting it to `0` means unlimited (preserving current behaviour).

### Problem or use case

On resource-constrained hardware with mechanical hard drives, unconstrained disk I/O during folder scans and sync saturates the drive completely, causing severe system-wide degradation.

Concrete example: Syncthing running in a container on a MikroTik router with an external USB HDD. When a folder scan starts, disk utilisation hits 100%, other containers stall, and the container watchdog kills Syncthing due to overall system unresponsiveness.

This is a common scenario on low-power embedded devices — MikroTik, OpenWrt routers, Raspberry Pi, home NAS — where Syncthing shares a slow HDD with other workloads. On such hardware even a single large folder scan can make the entire device unusable for the duration of the scan.

### Alternatives or workarounds

`maxFolderConcurrency` limits how many folders scan or sync simultaneously, which reduces peak disk load. However it does not bound the throughput of a single folder — one large folder can still saturate a slow HDD on its own.

Reducing the scan interval is another partial mitigation, but it does not help when the scan itself is the problem, only reduces how often it occurs.

No current option exists that directly limits the rate of disk I/O.

title: fix(fs): DirNames bypasses O_NOFOLLOW — symlink hardening incomplete after #10739
author: kapil971390
body:
## What happened

Commit #10739 added `O_NOFOLLOW` via `alwaysOpenFlags` to prevent symlinks from being followed as the final path component on Unix. However, `DirNames` in `lib/fs/basicfs.go` was not updated and still calls `os.OpenFile` directly instead of going through the `BasicFilesystem.OpenFile` wrapper where `alwaysOpenFlags` is applied:

\`\`\`go
// lib/fs/basicfs.go:224 — DirNames calls os.OpenFile directly, alwaysOpenFlags NOT applied
func (f *BasicFilesystem) DirNames(name string) ([]string, error) {
    name, err := f.rooted(name)
    ...
    fd, err := os.OpenFile(name, OptReadOnly, 0o777)  // ← O_NOFOLLOW missing
    ...
}

// lib/fs/basicfs.go:247 — OpenFile applies alwaysOpenFlags correctly
func (f *BasicFilesystem) OpenFile(name string, flags int, mode FileMode) (File, error) {
    ...
    flags |= alwaysOpenFlags  // ← O_NOFOLLOW applied here, but DirNames skips this
    fd, err := os.OpenFile(rootedName, flags, os.FileMode(mode))
    ...
}
\`\`\`

On Unix, `alwaysOpenFlags = syscall.O_NOFOLLOW` (from `lib/fs/basicfs_unix.go`). Because `DirNames` bypasses `f.OpenFile`, a symlink whose final component points to a directory will be silently followed, listing the contents of the symlink target instead of returning an error.

## What I expected to happen

`DirNames` should be consistent with `Open` / `OpenFile` / `Create` and refuse to follow a symlink as the final path component, returning an `ELOOP`/`ENOTDIR` error on Unix.

## Suggested fix

Replace the direct `os.OpenFile` call in `DirNames` with `f.OpenFile` so that `alwaysOpenFlags` is applied uniformly:

\`\`\`go
func (f *BasicFilesystem) DirNames(name string) ([]string, error) {
    fd, err := f.OpenFile(name, OptReadOnly, 0o777)
    if err != nil {
        return nil, err
    }
    defer fd.Close()
    ...
}
\`\`\`

This was identified via static analysis of the diff introduced by #10739.

## Operating system / version

Affects all Unix systems (Linux, macOS) where `alwaysOpenFlags = syscall.O_NOFOLLOW`. Windows is unaffected (`alwaysOpenFlags = 0`).

- OS: Linux / macOS
- Syncthing version: current main (identified in commit a5cbeea)

title: Non-promiscuous mode
author: 497e0bdf29873
body:
### Feature description

Reduce unnecessary network traffic: stop Syncthing from trying to do global discovery at a high frequency. A way to mark some devices as being mainly offline, and leave connection initiation up to those devices. 

### Problem or use case

I started exploring my network traffic, since my incompetent ISP started complaining about “tens of thousands of sessions”. It turns out that Syncthing is banging the global discovery servers every 60 seconds or so. This appears to be due to my iOS devices, that are only online when MöbiusSync is open, and an old laptop that is only rarely online. I tried to set `reconnectionIntervalS` to 600 seconds, but it does not seem to have any effect on the banging of the discovery server.

I then tried to make the address field of the mostly offline devices empty on the mostly online devices, leaving it up to the mostly offline devices to connect. This wasn't allowed (the field became `dynamic` nevertheless), so, as a workaround, I filled it with `tcp://127.0.0.1:12345`. This seemed to work on the local net: the banging stopped, and the devices still connected. I suppose this was through local discovery, because I can no longer connect externally through global discovery and relays.

So this feature, maybe `none` as address field, would, first of all, stop the constant global discovery search for devices that are unlikely to be found, but also include the necessary changes that those devices, alone, can initiate the connection through global discovery and relays. 





### Alternatives or workarounds

`tcp://127.0.0.1:12345` address field for the mostly offline devices. Worked on local net, but connections can no longer be initiated from outside the local net, for some reason.

title: setting cleanupIntervalS value via api call resets it to 1h
author: pokegamer5547
body:
### What happened?

When we set a folder to pause via api call, it resets the rescan interval and watch for changes values to default. It was reported in [10389](https://github.com/syncthing/syncthing/issues/10389)

Another value seems to reset too which is cleanupIntervalS, resets to 1h again whenever we send pause api call. 

So i thought to workaround by sending api call 
`curl -s -k -X PATCH -H "X-API-Key:xxxxx" -H "Content-Type: application/json" https://localhost:8384/rest/config/folders/{folderid} -d '{"cleanupIntervalS":86400}'`

But it's again resetting the value to 1h.
If i set rescanIntervalS via api call, that seems to reset cleanupIntervalS too back to 1h. 

`curl -s -k -X PATCH -H "X-API-Key:xxxxxx" -H "Content-Type: application/json" https://localhost:8384/rest/config/folders/{folderid} -d '{"rescanIntervalS":86400}'`

### Syncthing version

2.1.1

### Platform & operating system

Fedora Linux

### Browser version

_No response_

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

title: CLI tool
author: psion
body:
### Feature description

I run syncthing on different systems that I ssh into, and would rather not have the web gui open at all, password or not, to other systems.  Perhaps a CLI tool that I can use to add/remove folders, view and accept or reject share requests, etc.

### Problem or use case

I won't have to use the web gui often, especially over the network.

### Alternatives or workarounds

None yet.
